input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Living Lab case study of a building developed for the TwinLight and BOREALIS research projects, centred on illuminating control. The physical twin system is a BIM model of the building that has been enriched to include sensors, actuators, and controllers for lighting. Main components are lighting fixtures, illumination and occupancy sensors, dimming actuators and local control units. The operational domain is building energy and occupant‑comfort management with a need for continuous real‑time data exchange and dynamic control, making it an ideal candidate for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers real‑time monitoring of sensor data, automated control of actuators, immersive visualization through interactive dashboards, and data‑driven predictive analytics and optimization for energy efficiency and asset health. These services are available to building control engineers, facility managers, and DT engineers, enabling them to model devices, observe system states, and extend functionality via custom plugins.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven deployment framework that extends traditional BIM with device models (BCS components). Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts. Real‑time data exchange mechanisms keep physical devices and their twin synchronized, while an interactive visualization layer provides immersive interfaces. The framework’s modular architecture exposes standardized extension points for custom services and analytics, supported by the TwinLight and BOREALIS research projects.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system adopts a modular, service‑oriented architecture where a central Digital Twin Framework hub orchestrates bidirectional data exchange between enriched BIM models and the physical building’s sensor fleet, while exposing standardized extension points for custom analytics and service integration. Runtime configuration management allows the framework to adapt to evolving operational needs without full redeployment, providing dynamic orchestration and resource management across the Digital Twin lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system derives its computational models from enriched Building Information Models (BIM) that include sensor, actuator, and controller definitions. Using Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles, the framework validates, converts, and configures these BIM elements into structured representations that feed into the middleware layer. Geometric models are retained from the BIM, while behavioral and control logic are captured in validate‑conversion artifacts. Data from physical devices is streamed in real‑time to the Eclipse Hono broker and persisted in Eclipse Ditto’s entity database, where each device is represented as a Ditto Thing with associated state properties. The relationship between the BIM topology and the Ditto entities is maintained through conversion rules that map BIM element identifiers to Ditto Thing IDs. The Unreal Engine visualizer consumes the transformed models and the live property updates via Ditto’s real‑time API, enabling synchronized, immersive interaction. Data management is performed by Ditto’s persistent event store, guaranteeing durability, while Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery ensures reliable ingestion.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are defined directly within the enriched BIM model by the Building Control Engineer and are translated by the conversion component into corresponding Ditto Things. The actual control commands are routed through the DittoController component of the middleware and are ultimately delivered to the physical actuator via the dedicated device interface on the building side. The framework supports remote actuation by exposing actuator properties as writable attributes in Ditto; writes are validated against policy constraints before being sent to the physical device. Safety constraints are enforced at two levels: (1) policy‑based validation in Ditto that checks command ranges and units, preventing out‑of‑range or unsafe operations; (2) pre‑execution checks performed by the DittoController to avoid conflicts with concurrent commands. The framework’s modular extension points allow custom actuator behaviors to be added through FeatureParser components such as the LightFeatureParser, which interprets commands like turning on/off, setting brightness, and specifying color temperature in kelvin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are modeled in BIM and converted into Ditto Things with appropriate property schemas. Each sensor streams measurement data in real‑time to Eclipse Hono, which forwards it to Ditto via the DittoConnection component. The data flow is bidirectional: sensor readings are published to Ditto’s event channel, and corresponding updates are reflected in the UE Engine visualizer. The framework ensures data integrity by using Ditto’s policy engine to validate sensor values against defined ranges and units before persisting them. Although the documents do not specify sampling frequencies or spatial distribution, the process allows these parameters to be encoded in the BIM metadata and respected during conversion.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is preserved by validating enriched BIM models against a set of structural and semantic rules during the validation step of the conversion pipeline. Eclipse Ditto’s policy engine performs continuous verification of incoming sensor data and actuator commands, ensuring that all values respect predefined bounds and units. Fault tolerance is achieved through Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery guarantee and Ditto’s persistent event storage, providing resilience against network interruptions. Error detection is handled by policy violations that trigger alerts or message rejections, while error correction is achieved by either resetting the device state or reverting to the last stable state stored in Ditto. Quality assurance of the DT system is supported by the modular architecture, which enables adding custom analytics services to monitor fidelity metrics and enforce consistency between the physical assets and their digital twins.}
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data exchange between physical buildings and their digital twins is described. The framework ensures continuous synchronization; data types inferred to include sensor readings and BIM model updates. Specific protocols, frequencies or preprocessing steps are not explicitly detailed in the documents. The architecture supports bidirectional communication, but concrete mechanisms for data quality assurance and integration are omitted.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Facility managers use the system to issue control commands from digital twins to actual building equipment. Command types and message formats are not specified, and the documents lack details on validation, authentication, or feedback mechanisms. The framework is said to provide real‑time monitoring and control, but specific flow and conflict resolution details are absent.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The documents mention interoperability across heterogeneous systems and a modular architecture with standardized extension points. They do not provide concrete network protocols (e.g., MQTT, REST), topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability or security specifics for the Digital Twin network. Hence these details are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment infrastructure is broadly described: the DT Framework acts as a central hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange. The documents do not specify whether the deployment is on‑premise, edge or cloud, nor do they state computational, storage requirements, scalability or availability mechanisms. Therefore such details are not available.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Phases: 1) Initial device modeling directly within BIM, defining sensors, actuators, and controller logic; 2) Transformation via Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations; 3) Automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention; 4) Real‑time bidirectional data exchange ensuring continuous synchronization; 5) Interactive visualization and monitoring; 6) Extension of functionality through custom services via standardized extension points. Transitions are managed through automated artifact extraction and deployment pipelines, with governance enforced by verification against the enriched BIM baseline.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering begins with embedding device specifications inside the BIM model. The framework then employs MDE to transform the enriched BIM into a typed digital twin artefact in an automated fashion, minimizing manual setup. Runtime integrity is maintained by continuous two‑way data exchange that synchronizes sensor readings and actuator commands, automatically flagging any divergence between the physical and virtual states. Custom services can be added after deployment through modular extension points, allowing incremental evolution without regenerating the core twin model.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is delivered through the twin’s real‑time monitoring layer. Facility Managers interact with interactive visualization dashboards that display live sensor data, actuator status, and control‑logic states. The platform aggregates these data streams, enabling on‑the‑fly analytics such as trend detection or anomaly flagging. Alerts and suggestions are surfaced directly to the interface, allowing operators to drill into specific components, adjust control parameters, or trigger predefined corrective actions. The confidence of such recommendations is reinforced by the continuous telemetry synchronization between the physical building and the digital twin.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and specific to the Living Lab case study described in the source, correctly identifying the research projects (TwinLight and BOREALIS) and the focus on lighting control. It accurately lists physical components such as 'lighting fixtures, illumination and occupancy sensors, dimming actuators' and defines the operational domain. While it describes the 'physical twin system' as a 'BIM model' (which is actually the digital representation), the detailed description of the physical components and context is well-grounded in the text.

The extraction accurately lists the target users—'building control engineers, facility managers, and DT engineers'—exactly as defined in Table I. It correctly describes services such as 'real-time monitoring', 'immersive visualization', and 'custom plugins', supported by the Abstract's mention of 'rich, interactive visualization' and 'standardized extension points', and Section III's discussion of 'custom services'. The description of 'predictive analytics and optimization' is also supported by the introduction's mention of 'predictive analytics' and 'life cycle-encompassing optimization'.

The extraction is highly detailed and grounded in the text. It correctly identifies the 'model-driven deployment framework' and the use of 'Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations', quoting almost verbatim from Section III. It accurately lists the enabling technologies: 'real-time data exchange' (Eclipse Hono and Ditto in the Abstract), 'interactive visualization layer' (Unreal Engine in the Abstract), and the supporting projects 'TwinLight and BOREALIS' (Section II). The description of 'modular architecture' with 'standardized extension points' is also directly supported.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The description provides a comprehensive and accurate summary of the system's orchestration and architecture. It is strongly supported by the source text: 'Our platform features standardized extension points for modular service integration' and 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange'. The mention of 'Runtime configuration management' aligns with the text stating it 'enhances adaptability by enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment'.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. The source focuses on the internal architecture connecting BIM, Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and visualization tools. It does not describe integration protocols or interoperability with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins beyond the specific physical assets mentioned.

in the IFC model. Specifically, the LightTwinComponent

architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs

Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from

users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange' and 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment'.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
